text: The surgeon frees the foreshortened mesentery, which is adherent to the small bowel and right colon mesentery, to better expose the transverse colon by reflecting it upwards. The duodenum is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon frees the foreshortened mesentery, which is adherent to the small bowel and right colon mesentery, to better expose the transverse colon by reflecting it upwards.

The surgeon identifies the duodenum and approaches the ileocolic pedicle, entering the retroperitoneal plane over the duodenum. The mesentery is opened along the anticipated line of the superior mesenteric vessels, and the ileocolic pedicle is thinned out.

The surgeon approaches the ileocolic pedicle, entering the retroperitoneal plane over the duodenum. The mesentery is opened along the anticipated line of the superior mesenteric vessels. The ileocolic pedicle is thinned out, and the ileocolic vein is identified and divided, followed by oscillation of the ileocolic artery.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vein and divides it, relieving tension in the area. The ileocolic artery is then oscillated. The absence of a right colic vessel originating from the superior mesenteric vessels is noted, and the surgeon plans to address the middle colic artery due to the presence of a proximal transverse colon polyp with high-grade dysplasia.

The surgeon examines the foreshortened mesentery on the CAT scan, noting the absence of a right colic vessel originating from the superior mesenteric vessels. The transverse colon is adherent to the root of the small bowel mesentery. The surgeon marks the mesentery from the planned margin down to the middle colic vessels, preparing to isolate them for transection beyond the tattoo marking, which is six centimeters distal to the polyp.

The surgeon works to unfold the foreshortened mesentery where the transverse colon is adherent to the root of the small bowel mesentery, aiming to expose the transverse colon for proper margin selection. The tattoo marking is visible approximately six centimeters distal to the polyp, guiding the surgeon's transection plan. The mesentery is marked from the planned margin down to the middle colic vessels, which the surgeon begins to isolate.

The surgeon identifies a lymph node with black dye at the base of the middle colic vessels and thins out the surrounding fatty tissue. The artery and vein are taken together, and the transverse colon mesentery is dissected, revealing marginal vessels along the bowel wall. The medial dissection is completed, and the omentum is split at the planned transection level, with the omentum taken to the specimen side of the colon. The surgeon enters the previously dissected medial plane and proceeds around the hepatocolic ligament, noting adhesion of the omentum to the gallbladder.

The surgeon identifies a lymph node with black dye at the base of the middle colic vessels and thins out the surrounding fatty tissue. The artery and vein are taken together, and the transverse colon mesentery is dissected, revealing the marginal vessels along the bowel wall. The medial dissection is completed, and the surgeon splits the omentum at the planned transection level, moving it to the specimen side of the colon. The dissection continues over the bowel wall, entering the previously dissected medial plane, and proceeds around the hepatocolic ligament, with some omentum adhered to the gallbladder.

The surgeon navigates around the flexure with the camera positioned in the left lower quadrant, revealing the appendix. The view is adjusted to a sub-SQL perspective to better visualize the previously dissected medial plane. The surgeon then takes the lateral attachments of the cecum, part of the hepatocolic ligament, and checks perfusion before using a stapler on the colon side while observing the ICG. The small bowel is divided to the left of the falciform ligament.

The surgeon navigates around the flexure with the camera positioned in the left lower quadrant, initially struggling to obtain a clear view. The appendix is visible, aiding in joining the previously dissected medial plane. The surgeon then takes the lateral attachments of the cecum and the pedicolic ligament. The small bowel margin is taken, and perfusion is checked before using a stapler on the colon side while observing the ICG. The procedure is performed to the left of the falciform ligament, and the small bowel is divided.

The surgeon uses a stapler from the suprapubic port to create an isoparastaltic anastomosis to the patient's left of the falciform ligament. The stapler is positioned to achieve a precise anastomosis without needing to close an additional twelve millimeter port site. The common channel is closed starting from the inside to the outside at the near corner.

The surgeon uses a stapler from the suprapubic port to create an isoparastaltic anastomosis to the patient's left of the falciform ligament. The common channel is closed starting from the inside to the outside at the near corner, using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, with the far corner lifted by the surgeon's left hand. The surgeon checks ICG perfusion and imprecates the top of the common channel back down.

The surgeon places a transition stitch to secure the anastomosis, lifting the far corner with the left hand. Indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence is checked to ensure adequate perfusion. A 3-0 V-Loc suture is used to imbricate the top of the common channel, ensuring a second layer covers the corner.
